topic: Agentic AI for reliable academic literature review and hallucination mitigation in large language models
final_review:
The integration of Agentic AI in academic literature review has gained significant attention in recent years, with a focus on reliable and efficient analysis of large volumes of data (Olaf Zawacki‐Richter, Victoria I. Marín, Melissa Bond et al., 2019). The application of artificial intelligence in higher education is not without its challenges, and there is a need for educators to be more involved in the development and implementation of AI-based systems (Olaf Zawacki‐Richter, Victoria I. Marín, Melissa Bond et al., 2019).

The use of large language models has been explored in various studies, with some highlighting their potential to revolutionize education (Enkelejda Kasneci, Kathrin Seßler, Stefan Küchemann et al., 2023; Yogesh K. Dwivedi, Nir Kshetri, Laurie Hughes et al., 2023). However, others have raised concerns about the challenges and implications of using generative conversational AI, including the potential for hallucination and the need for explainability (Yogesh K. Dwivedi, Nir Kshetri, Laurie Hughes et al., 2023; Alejandro Barredo Arrieta, Natalia Díaz-Rodríguez, Javier Del Ser et al., 2019).

Recent surveys and technical reports have provided insights into the capabilities and limitations of large language models, including GPT-4 (OpenAI, Wijayagunawardhana, W. A. Hasindu N., 2023; Wayne Xin Zhao, Kun Zhou, Junyi Li et al., 2023). These studies have highlighted the need for further research into the development of autonomous agents and the mitigation of hallucination in large language models (Lei Wang, Chen Ma, Xueyang Feng et al., 2024).

Despite the potential benefits of Agentic AI in academic literature review, there is a need for more research into the opportunities and challenges of using large language models in education (Jürgen Rudolph, Samson Tan, Shannon Tan, 2023). The use of explainable AI and techniques such as Grad-CAM can help to address some of the challenges associated with large language models (Alejandro Barredo Arrieta, Natalia Díaz-Rodríguez, Javier Del Ser et al., 2019; Ramprasaath R. Selvaraju, Michael Cogswell, Abhishek Das et al., 2019).

References:
Alejandro Barredo Arrieta, N. D.-R., J. D. S., et al. (2019) Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): Concepts, taxonomies, opportunities and challenges toward responsible AI.
Enkelejda Kasneci, K. S., S. K., et al. (2023) ChatGPT for good? On opportunities and challenges of large language models for education.
Jürgen Rudolph, S. T., S. T. (2023) ChatGPT: Bullshit spewer or the end of traditional assessments in higher education?
Lei Wang, C. M., X. F., et al. (2024) A survey on large language model based autonomous agents.
Olaf Zawacki‐Richter, V. I. M., M. B., et al. (2019) Systematic review of research on artificial intelligence applications in higher education – where are the educators?
OpenAI, W. A. H. N. (2023) GPT-4 Technical Report.
Ramprasaath R. Selvaraju, M. C., A. D., et al. (2019) Grad-CAM: Visual Explanations from Deep Networks via Gradient-Based Localization.
Wayne Xin Zhao, K. Z., J. L., et al. (2023) A Survey of Large Language Models.
Yogesh K. Dwivedi, N. K., L. H., et al. (2023) Opinion Paper: “So what if ChatGPT wrote it?” Multidisciplinary perspectives on opportunities, challenges and implications of generative conversational AI for research, practice and policy.
